This makes sense. That test takes a `pretty_midi.PrettyMIDI` object which has notes at start times `[1, 2, 3, ..., 9]` and remaps them as `pm.adjust_times([0, 5, 5, 10], [5, 10, 12, 17])`.  In this new format `np.unique([0, 5, 5, 10])` returns indices `[0, 1, 3]` which correspond to new times`[5, 10, 17]`. The previous behavior of the code mapped 5 to 12, now it maps 5 to 10. Neither feels obviously correct to me so as long as it's documented I think it's fine. You should be able to fix the test by changing https://github.com/craffel/pretty-midi/blob/master/tests/test_pretty_midi.py#L165 to `pm.adjust_times([0, 5, 5, 10], [5, 12, 13, 17])`. Does that work?

Though supported, the `getargspec(urlopen).args` in python3 does not contain `context`. That is because of a `*` in `urllib.request.urlopen`'s argspec [1]. Whereas, `urllib2.urlopen` does not have that[2]. In my testing, the current logic only worked for python2 and not for python3.

Here's an example snippet that shows the difference in behavior:

```python
try:
    from inspect import getfullargspec as getargspec
except ImportError:
    from inspect import getargspec

try:
    from urllib.request import urlopen
except ImportError:
    from urllib2 import urlopen

import pprint


pprint.pprint(getargspec(urlopen).args)
```

Running the above snippet does this on my machine:

```shell
# Python2
$ python ./test.py 
['url', 'data', 'timeout', 'cafile', 'capath', 'cadefault', 'context']

# Python3
$ python3 ./test.py 
['url', 'data', 'timeout']
```

[1] https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.6/Lib/urllib/request.py#L141
[2] https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Lib/urllib2.py#L131

`Exception` is probably too general. Use `socket.error` specifically. Using `Exception` could masquerade bugs in our own code (e.g., AttributeError, ValueError, TypeError, etc.). `socket.error` is the base class for the other socket exceptions. See "echo client program" example in https://docs.python.org/2/library/socket.html

This was pretty rough. Here's what's going on.

Django's test client doesn't set `request.user` before merrily jaunting off into yonder field of daisies and Django's `RequestFactory` doesn't go through middleware at all.

Since we really want a few tests that test handling a request with the mechanics of the middleware to verify that the Django 1.10+ transitional mixin worked with our middleware correctly, so I was forced to do this weirdo thing where we set our own `ClientHandler` that adds a `.user` to the request before passing it through the machinery.

Another way to do this might be to switch to webtest, but I was too emotionally drained to see what shenanigans I would have to pull from my magic hat to get that vaguely working.

So that's why it looks so wonky. I tried to add lots of comments to alleviate the weirdness. Let me know if it's still unclear.

Also, I *hate* how Django's test client `.login` fails silently. That's so irritating and I lost at least an hour tinkering with code that was fine trying to debug it. In what bizarre and paranormal circumstances would I ever want it to fail silently, I cannot begin to fathom.

The intent of these test fixtures is to address how this can caught in the future.  That is, how can people know they need to use `BaseSessionTest` or face potential travis failures?  If you have credentials configured in your dev setup (very likely) you'll never see this issue until travis runs the tests.  This forces the issue locally by setting all the normal cred env vars to bogus values so you'll see similar behavior to what travis sees.

The project homepage link takes on increasing prominence in Warehouse (the PyPI successor), being placed right at the top. It makes sense to me that a visitor from warehouse would click the link to get to documentation about the project, not a github page that doesn't really tell them how to use the package. 

I do agree that whatever that link goes, you should be able to arrive at the alternate location easily. In other projects, I've handled this by having the RTD homepage have a direct link to the github project in a 'development' section (where I also put the CI badges). Having a RTD badge in README.rst can also accomplish this, but it's one more step for a user viewing the PyPI page and looking for documentation.

These are merged, these comments are for future reference to account for the changes I made during merge.

* This one is only used internally. As far as I know there is no public API that uses this and there won't be a `.type` property on Header, so I removed the example.
* The alias, if there is one, is the part of the MS member names that's the same for each member. In this case it's WdHeaderFooter -> WD_HEADER_FOOTER
* Enum modules get a docstring, as does every module.
* The description string for each member is generally copied directly from the MS API web page, adapted only when necessary. This one requires quite a bit of interpolation since it's used a bit differently and the MS ones are misleading and in cases just plain wrong :)
* The enumeration documentation page is generated automatically from the enumeration definition like this:

  ```python
  >>> from docx.enum.header import WD_HEADER_FOOTER as WD
  >>> print WD.__docs_rst__
```

```rst
.. _WdHeaderFooterIndex:

``WD_HEADER_FOOTER_INDEX``
==========================

alias: **WD_HEADER_FOOTER**

Specifies the type of a header or footer.

----

PRIMARY
    The header or footer appearing on all pages except when an even and/or
    first-page header/footer is defined.

FIRST_PAGE
    The header or footer appearing only on the first page of the specified
    section.

EVEN_PAGES
    The header or footer appearing on even numbered (verso) pages in the
    specified section.
```

This text is used to create the documentation page you can see in the merged commit (along with it's index entry).

I see what you mean -- IMO the data attribute stuff is kind of unavoidable. I suspect it's the kind of thing going on behind the scenes for forms in any framework, but you're right that here it's our responsibility to manage the complexity. 

As for onBeforeRender, that's necessitated to keep the form "nice" on rerender, kind of in line with what you had mentioned about input elements losing focus on re-render. In this case, it's so that intermediary values are kept even when validation fails.

The trade-off is whether the global app state should granularly manage the state of each form (i.e. have a disabled attribute for each field, a focus attribute, an attribute representing the value of each input element) vs hiding many of the details by managing some state in the form view itself. Since they're highly interactive, forms seem to experience a "state explosion". Moreover any element that deals with user input I think will face similar issues in terms of reacting to user's actions -- should user input be modeled as a change of global state, or do we introduce local state with the aim of simplifying global state?

Unfortunately, **we haven't been able to reach a consensus** here yet. In fact, after more extensive IRC discussion, I'm pretty sure **this should not be merged** here.

In general, I strongly believe that:

1. `le-auto` should not impose any constraints on `setup.py`. By the way, how would you impose such constraints on `setup.py` files found external dependencies?

2. Up so far, we have kept our tests together with the main source code. As long as we do it, we should also have test deps in `install_requires`, because that code is shipped to end-users and importable. Rationale for such setup is not really in the scope of this PR, but I'm open to discussion elsewhere. Moreover, messing with this is likely introduce more bugs. That particular concern Erik brought up, was the quickest argument I could come up with, in that particular context.

An obvious solution here is to have `le-auto` pin `"mock==1.1.0"` as this would satisfy `acme`'s setup.py constraints under both Python 2.6 and 2.7. Implementing this plan is about adding one new line to pin mock in `conditional_requirements.py` and one (or more) to add it's hash (anyway it must be easy - it was meant to be this way, because we're likely often to update those pins and their hashes - that's the whole point of this PR). Touching `setup.py` is really the wrong hammer...

Consider using `np.testing.assert_allclose` instead of this, which is robust to implementation changes that yield slightly different results due to IEEE 754. Then, you can just write `2/3` instead of `0.6666666865348816`:
```py
In [10]: np.testing.assert_allclose(0.6666666865348816, 2/3)

In [11]: np.testing.assert_allclose(1.6666666865348816, 2/3)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
[…]
```

I'm not sure the client gets to specify what prefixes it's interested in receiving IPv6 addresses for. Nothing in RFC 3315 specifies that behavior. A simple DHCP server implementation will just assign addresses for all configured prefixes to the client. A single IA_NA Option can carry multiple IAADDR options. I think it would be simpler and more efficient to just return all addresses within a single DHCPv6 Reply.
